title: Algebra Basics
blurb: Master expressions, FOIL, special products, and factoring — the engine behind GRE Quant.
theory:
What Algebra Is

Algebra uses letters called variables to stand for unknown numbers. A term is a number, a variable, or a product of them (for example 5, x, or 3x2). A coefficient is the number multiplying a variable (in 3x2 the coefficient is 3). An algebraic expression joins terms with + and − signs, such as 3x2 − 5x + 2.

Combining Like Terms

Like terms have the exact same variable part (same letters with the same exponents). Only like terms can be added or subtracted; you simply add their coefficients.

  • 7x − 2x = 5x (like)
  • 4x2 + 3x2 = 7x2 (like)
  • 5x + 3y stays as is (unlike — different variables)
  • x2 + x stays as is (unlike — different exponents)

The Distributive Property

This is the most-used rule in all of algebra: a(b + c) = ab + ac. Multiply the outside factor by every term inside the parentheses.

  • 3(2x − 5) = 6x − 15
  • −2(x − 4) = −2x + 8 (watch the signs)
  • x(x + 7) = x2 + 7x
MistakeCorrect
−(x − 3) = −x − 3−(x − 3) = −x + 3

Evaluating and Substituting

To evaluate an expression, replace each variable with its given value, then follow order of operations (PEMDAS). Always put substituted negatives in parentheses.

Example: evaluate 2x2 − 3 when x = 4. Then 2(4)2 − 3 = 2(16) − 3 = 32 − 3 = 29.

Multiplying Binomials: FOIL

A binomial has two terms. To multiply two binomials use FOIL — First, Outer, Inner, Last:

  • (x + 3)(x + 5): First x·x = x2; Outer x·5 = 5x; Inner 3·x = 3x; Last 3·5 = 15.
  • Combine: x2 + 5x + 3x + 15 = x2 + 8x + 15.

Special Products (Memorize These)

These three patterns appear constantly on the GRE and let you skip FOIL entirely:

  • (a + b)2 = a2 + 2ab + b2
  • (a − b)2 = a2 − 2ab + b2
  • (a + b)(a − b) = a2 − b2 (difference of squares)

So (2x − 3)2 = 4x2 − 12x + 9, and (3x + 2)(3x − 2) = 9x2 − 4. A frequent trap: (a + b)2 is not a2 + b2 — the middle term 2ab matters.

Factoring: Undoing Multiplication

Factoring rewrites a sum as a product. Three core methods:

1) Greatest Common Factor (GCF). Pull the largest factor shared by every term. 6x3 − 9x2 = 3x2(2x − 3). Always check for a GCF first.

2) Difference of Squares. a2 − b2 = (a + b)(a − b). So x2 − 9 = (x + 3)(x − 3), and 49 − x2 = (7 + x)(7 − x). This works only for a difference, never a sum.

3) Trinomials. For x2 + bx + c, find two numbers that multiply to c and add to b. For x2 + 7x + 12, those numbers are 3 and 4, so it factors to (x + 3)(x + 4). When the leading coefficient is not 1, as in 2x2 + 7x + 3, look for factors of the form (2x + 1)(x + 3) by testing how the outer and inner products combine.

Simplifying Rational Expressions

A rational expression is a fraction with polynomials on top and bottom. To simplify, factor numerator and denominator completely, then cancel common factors — never cancel individual terms.

  • (x2 − 4)/(x + 2) = (x + 2)(x − 2)/(x + 2) = x − 2
  • (x2 + 5x + 6)/(x2 − 4) = (x + 2)(x + 3)/[(x + 2)(x − 2)] = (x + 3)/(x − 2)

You may cancel a whole factor like (x + 2), but you may not cancel the x in (x + 3)/x — addition blocks cancellation.
whatEtsTests:
• FOIL and special products both directions — expanding (a±b)2 and (a+b)(a−b), and recognizing them backwards to factor instantly.
  • Factoring to simplify — most algebra questions reduce to factoring a quadratic or canceling a rational expression.
  • Quantitative Comparison with expressions — comparing two algebraic quantities where you simplify or plug in smart values (0, 1, negatives, fractions).
  • Substitution and clean-up — evaluating messy expressions where simplifying first avoids arithmetic errors.
  • Sign control — distributing negatives and subtracting grouped terms correctly.
  • Difference of squares as a shortcut — turning products like 51×49 into 502 − 12.
tips: Always factor out the GCF first — it makes every later step (factoring, canceling) easier.
Recognize a<sup>2</sup> − b<sup>2</sup> to compute products fast: 51×49 = 50<sup>2</sup> − 1 = 2499.
For x<sup>2</sup> + bx + c, find two numbers that multiply to c and add to b — then write them directly.
On QC and abstract problems, plug in 0, 1, −1, and a fraction to test whether quantities are equal or which is larger.
Wrap every substituted value in parentheses so negatives and exponents behave correctly.
To square a binomial, write a<sup>2</sup>, then 2ab, then b<sup>2</sup> — never forget the middle term.
traps: Writing (a + b)<sup>2</sup> = a<sup>2</sup> + b<sup>2</sup> and dropping the 2ab middle term.
Distributing a negative to only the first term: −(x − 3) becomes −x − 3 instead of −x + 3.
Canceling individual terms in a fraction, e.g. (x + 3)/x = 3, instead of canceling whole factors.
Trying to factor a sum of squares like x<sup>2</sup> + 9 as a difference of squares — it does not factor over the reals.
Combining unlike terms, such as treating x<sup>2</sup> + x as 2x<sup>2</sup> or 2x.

examples.steps:
1. Distribute the first term: 3(2x − 4) = 6x − 12.
  2. Distribute the second, keeping the negative: −2(x − 5) = −2x + 10.
  3. Combine: 6x − 12 − 2x + 10.
  4. Like terms: (6x − 2x) + (−12 + 10) = 4x − 2.

Answer: 4x − 2.

1. Use (a − b)2 = a2 − 2ab + b2 with a = 2x, b = 5.
  2. a2 = (2x)2 = 4x2.
  3. 2ab = 2(2x)(5) = 20x, so the middle term is −20x.
  4. b2 = 25.

Answer: 4x2 − 20x + 25.
examples.shortcut: Square the ends (4x2, 25), then drop in twice the product with the sign of the binomial: −20x.

examples.steps:
1. Need two numbers that multiply to −15 and add to +2.
  2. Test pairs: 5 and −3 give product −15 and sum +2. ✓
  3. Write as (x + 5)(x − 3).

Answer: (x + 5)(x − 3).
examples.shortcut: Since the product is negative, the numbers have opposite signs; the larger-magnitude one (5) takes the sign of the middle term (+).

examples.steps:
1. Factor the numerator as a difference of squares: x2 − 9 = (x + 3)(x − 3).
  2. Factor the trinomial: two numbers multiplying to 12 and adding to 7 are 3 and 4, so x2 + 7x + 12 = (x + 3)(x + 4).
  3. Cancel the common factor (x + 3).

Answer: (x − 3)/(x + 4).
examples.shortcut: Both top and bottom share (x + 3); spot it and cancel to land on (x − 3)/(x + 4) immediately.

questions.explain: −4 · x = −4x and −4 · (−6) = +24. So the result is −4x + 24. The negative must hit both terms.
The GCF of 6x3 and 9x2 is 3x2. Dividing gives 6x3/3x2 = 2x and 9x2/3x2 = 3. Result: 3x2(2x − 3).
FOIL: First x2; Outer −7x; Inner +4x; Last −28. Middle: −7x + 4x = −3x. Result: x2 − 3x − 28.
Test (2x + 1)(x + 3): Outer 6x, Inner x, sum 7x; Last 1·3 = 3; First 2x2. Gives 2x2 + 7x + 3. ✓ Answer: (2x + 1)(x + 3).

questions.q: If (a + b)2 = 49 and a2 + b2 = 25, what is the value of ab?
questions.explain: (a + b)2 = a2 + 2ab + b2. So 49 = 25 + 2ab, giving 2ab = 24 and ab = 12.

questions.q: x is any real number. Compare the two quantities.
questions.explain: By the difference-of-squares pattern, (3x + 2)(3x − 2) = (3x)2 − 22 = 9x2 − 4, which equals Quantity B for every x. The two quantities are always equal.

questions.q: x is a real number. Compare the two quantities.
questions.explain: (x + 3)2 = x2 + 6x + 9, so A − B = 6x. If x > 0, A > B; if x < 0, A < B; if x = 0, they are equal. The relationship varies, so the answer is cannot be determined.

questions.q: For which of the following values of x is the expression (x2 − 5x + 6)/(x2 − 4) equal to 0? Select all that apply.
questions.explain: Factor: (x − 2)(x − 3)/[(x − 2)(x + 2)] = (x − 3)/(x + 2) for x ≠ 2, −2. The value is 0 only when the numerator (x − 3) = 0, i.e. x = 3. At x = 2 the original expression is undefined (0/0), so it does not equal 0. Only x = 3 works.

questions.q: If x2 − y2 = 40 and x + y = 8, what is the value of x − y?
questions.explain: x2 − y2 = (x + y)(x − y). So 40 = 8(x − y), giving x − y = 40/8 = 5.
